title: Four-shaft herringbone twill
subtitle: A reversing twill that produces a V-pattern cloth on a four-shaft floor loom
excerpt: Herringbone twill reverses direction every eight to sixteen threads to produce a chevron pattern across the cloth. The structure uses the same threading and tie-up as a standard 2/2 twill but with the threading sequence mirrored at regular intervals. The resulting cloth has the diagonal texture of twill but with a woven-in pattern that does not require colour change.
sourceNotes: Herringbone twill is documented in Hooper, Hand-Loom Weaving (1910) and in J. Tovey, The Technique of Weaving (1965, Batsford). The threading and treadling sequences described are standard four-shaft herringbone as reproduced in UK weaving curricula.

glossaryTerms.definition: A threading sequence that reverses direction at regular intervals to create a mirrored diagonal pattern in the cloth. A standard herringbone uses the sequence 1-2-3-4-3-2-1-2-3-4-3-2 repeating, producing a chevron or V-pattern. The reversal point is the pivot thread where the sequence turns back on itself.

Any twill threading that departs from the straight 1-2-3-4 draw by introducing reversals, skips, or point repeats. Herringbone is a point-reversing broken twill. Broken twills produce geometric patterns in the weave structure without requiring more than four shafts.

content.text: : the threading sequence reverses direction at regular intervals using a

sequence rather than a straight draw. The reversal creates a V or chevron shape in the diagonal lines of the twill weave. This entry covers a wool or wool-blend herringbone for a scarf or sampler cloth.

The threading sequence is 1-2-3-4-3-2 repeating, which reverses at shafts 4 and 1. The treadling follows a straight progression 1-2-3-4 then reverses to 4-3-2-1, creating a herringbone pattern in both threading and treadling directions. The reversal points in both threading and treadling must align for the chevron to appear cleanly.

items.name: DK or worsted wool warp yarn
DK or worsted wool weft yarn (same or matching colour)
Four-shaft floor loom, at least 30 cm weaving width

content.text: Set DK wool at 8 ends per 10 cm (one end per dent in an 8-dent reed). Thread following the herringbone sequence: 1-2-3-4-3-2, repeat. Count threads carefully at each reversal point; a threading error at the pivot produces a step in the chevron rather than a sharp point. Use a threading hook and count each group of six threads (one repeat) before moving to the next. Tie up as shown in the draft.

Treadle in sequence 1-2-3-4, then reverse to 4-3-2-1 and repeat. The reversal point in the treadling should mirror the reversal point in the threading. If the threading reverses every 6 threads, the treadling typically reverses every 8 picks to give a near-square herringbone cell. Adjust the number of picks per block to change the proportion of the chevron.

items.problem: The chevron has a step rather than a sharp point at the reversal
items.cause: The threading or treadling reversal count is off by one thread
items.fix: Recount the threading at the reversal point and confirm the pivot thread. The pivot shaft (1 or 4) should appear once at the reversal, not twice.

items.problem: The herringbone pattern is not visible until after wet-finishing
items.cause: Wool cloth needs wet-finishing to bloom and set the weave structure
items.fix: This is normal: wet-finish the cloth before judging the pattern. Wool herringbone typically becomes clearly visible only after washing and pressing.
